in   ebrew   shortly   after   the   beginning   of
the   hristian   era,   this   book   was   designed   by   its
author   to protest   against   the   growing   secularisation
of   the   harisaic   party   through   its   fusion   with
political   ideals   and   popular   essianic   beliefs.   ts
 author,   a   harisaic   uietist,   sought   herein   to recall
 his   party   to   the   old   paths,   which   they   were   fast
forsaking,   of   simple   unobtrusive   obedience   to   the
aw.   e   glorifies,   accordingly,   the   old   ideals
which   had   been   cherished   and   pursued   by   the
hasid   and   arly   harisaic   party,   but   which   the
harisaism   of   the   first   century   .c.   had   begun   to
disown   in   favour   of   a   more   active   réle in the   life
of   the   nation.   e   foresaw,   perhaps,   the   doom   to
which   his   country   was   hurrying   under   such   a
shortsighted   and   unspiritual   policy,   and   laboured
with   all   his   power   to   stay   its downward   progress.
ut   all in vain.   e   but   played   afresh   the   part   of
